                 EMPLOYMENT
                 AND
                 EARNINGS
                  AND MONTHLY
                  REPORT ON                                                             MARCH 1968
                  THE LABOR FORCE                                                     VOL. 14 NO. 9
                  JOSEPH M. F1NERTY, EDITOR   KATHRYN D, HOYLE, ASSOCIATE EDITOR


                                                       Highlights
                         A timely article by Paul Ryscavage and Hazel Willacy reviews
                           recent developments in manufacturing (page l l ) .
                         DarrellNelson explains the BLS earnings series as a guide for
                           users in escalation agreements (page 18).
                         Final 1967 averages for establishment-based data are shown in
                           all tables containing national series in sections B, C and D .
                         Additional tables of revised seasonally adjusted labor force series
                           appear in this issue (page 22).
                         Employment data for the Salem, Oregon, Standard Metropolitan
                           Statistical Area,    are shown for the first time in table B-7
                           (page 88).
                         A list of articles p u b l i s h e d in Employment and Earnings and
                           Monthly Report on the Labor Force since January 1966 is
                           shown on page 140.

                                    FECENT DEVELOPMENTS IN MANUFACTURING
                                                  by Paul M. Ryscavage and Hazel M. Willacy *



           The 1967 economic slowdown had a rela-                    trial production and employment. As a
        tively greater impact on manufacturing em-                   result, manufacturing employment recorded
        ployees than on any other group of workers.jy                its smallest gain since 1963.
        Manufacturing jobs rose by only 150,000 in
        1967, compared to 1.1 million in 1966 and an                 Anatomy of a Job Gain
        average annual increase of nearly 600,000 in
        the 1962-66 period. In contrast, 1967 job                       Not only was the 1967 job gain in manu-
        gains in the service producing industries ex-                facturing smaller than that registered in
        ceeded their average increase over the last                  previous years of the expansion, but it was
        4 years.                                                     also considerably different in nature. The
                                                                     impact of the economic pause on the indi-
            The inventory-sales imbalance which                      vidual industries was unequal, and,as out-
         occurred in the first-half of the year struck               lined below, the nature of the job gain was
         hardest in the manufacturing sector as                      vastly different from that in previous years.
         manufacturers cut both employment and
         hours in order to adjust their inventory
         positions. In addition, several major work                   (1) The increase in manufacturing jobs
         stoppages, a leveling off in capital invest-                     occurred among nonproductionwork-
         ment, weaker demand for automobiles, and                         ers, unlike the 1962-66 period when
         a slower increase in defense spending com-                       job gains were registered for both
         bined to exert an additional drag on indus-                      production and nonproduction work-

                                                       Technical Note

                                           Additional information concerning the preparation
                                           of the labor force, employment, hours and earnings,
                                           and labor turnover series—concepts and scope,
                                           survey methods, and limitations—is contained in
                                           technical notes for each of these series, available
                                           from the Bureau of Labor Statistics free of charge.

       and salary employment, hours, earnings, and labor turn-            respondent each month so that the next month's data can

       graphic location.                                                  parability and accuracy of reporting, since the r e -

       the respondent fills out only one employment or labor              ber of full- and part-time workers on the payrolls of non-
       turnover schedule, which is then used for national, State,         agricultural establishments and, for most industries,
       and area estimates. This eliminates duplicate reporting            payroll and man-hours of production and related workers
       on the part of respondents and, together with the use of           or nonsupervisory workers for the pay period which
       identical techniques at the national and State levels,             includes the 12th of the month. The labor turnover

       and examine the returns for consistency, accuracy, and

        Design
                                                                       Coverage
           The sampling plan used in the current employment
       statistics program is an optimum allocation design known            The BLS sample of establishment employment and
       as "sampling proportionate to average size of establish-        payrolls is the largest monthly sampling operation in the
       ment." The universe of establishments is stratified first       field of social statistics. The table that follows shows
       by industry and then within each industry by size of            the approximate proportion of total employment in each
       establishment in terms of employment. For each industry         industry division covered by the group of establishments
       the total size of the sample is distributed among the size      furnishing monthly employment data. The coverage for
       class cells on the basis of average employment per              individual industries within the division may vary from
       establishment in each cell. In practice, this is equivalent     the proportions shown.
       to distributing the predetermined total number of estab-
       lishments required in the sample among the cells on the
       basis of the ratio of employment in each cell to total              Approximate size and coverage of BLS employment
       employment in the industry. Within each stratum the                        and payrolls sample, March 1966 x
       sample members are selected at random.

            Under this type of design, large establishments fall                                               Employees
        into the sample with certainty. The size of the samples                Industry division
        for the various industries is determined empirically                                               Number       Percent
        on the basis of experience and of cost considerations.                                             reported     of total

        total employment is concentrated in relatively few

        is included in the sample. Consequently, the sample

                                                          ATTENTION

             As discussed in the Technical Note, the Bureau periodically adjusts the industry employ-
       ment series to a recent benchmark to improve their accuracy. These adjustments may also
       affect the hours and earnings series because employment levels are used as weights. Industry
       data for all national series shown in this report have been adjusted to March 1966 benchmarks.
       Data from April 1966 forward are subject to revision at the time of the next benchmark.

             Beginning with the September 1967 and subsequent issues of Employment and Earnings
       and Monthly Report on the Labor Force, the national data in sections B, C, and D supersede
       those published in previous issues, as well as those appearing in the Handbook of Labor Statis-
       tics, 1967. Comparable data are published in Employment and Earnings Statistics for the
       United States, 1909-67, BLS Bulletin 1312-5.       "~"                  '                     ~

                 Industry titles conform to the Bureau of the Budget's standard list of short SIG titles.
